title: SDL Operation
description: An Operation is the unit of work inside a service — what the service actually does when an EntryPoint fires. It implements three DL concepts simultaneously: Unit (the actor), Flow (the ordered execution), and Perspective (multiple named views of the same logic). Every Operation has two required sibling blocks: 'business' (the intent perspective in plain language) and 'routing' (the implementation perspective in technical terms). These describe the same logic from different angles. The renderer toggles between them; no information is lost in either view.

perspectives.name: business
perspectives.description: Describes the operation in plain language for domain experts. Business rules, preconditions, outcomes, and failure modes.

perspectives.name: routing
perspectives.description: Describes the technical execution path for engineers. Handler function, middleware chain, ordered steps, and error handling.

dl.notes: Operation = Unit (performs work) + Flow (routing.steps[] is the ordered execution sequence) + Perspective (business{} and routing{} are the two named views).

id.description: Unique kebab-case identifier for this operation within the service.

label.description: Human-readable name. Write as a verb phrase.

description.description: Optional one-paragraph summary of what this operation does and why it exists.

business.description: The intent perspective on this operation. Describes what the service does from a business or domain standpoint, in plain language. No implementation details. Audience: domain experts, product managers, senior engineers auditing AI-generated logic.

summary.description: One or two sentences: what business goal does this operation accomplish?
examples: Accepts a customer's order request, validates it against business rules, and initiates the payment and fulfilment process.
Transitions an order to CONFIRMED status once payment has been verified.

rules.description: The business rules and domain decisions that govern this operation. Write each rule as a plain-language statement that a domain expert would articulate. These are what engineers need to understand and audit.
examples: An order cannot be created if any line item is out of stock.
Orders over $10,000 are automatically flagged for manual review before confirmation.
A customer may not place more than 5 orders within any 24-hour window.
The cart must contain at least one item with a positive quantity.

preconditions.description: What must be true before this operation can run — actor state, data requirements, authentication.

outcomes.description: Observable state changes after this operation succeeds. Written in plain language — not HTTP codes, not technical side effects.
examples: Order is persisted with AWAITING_PAYMENT status
Customer receives an order confirmation number

failure_modes.description: Meaningful failure cases from a business perspective — what goes wrong for the user or domain, and how the system responds.

examples: Order is rejected; customer is shown which items are unavailable
Order is rejected; customer is told when they can next place an order

domain_events_emitted.description: Domain events produced by this operation on success. Written in past-tense verb.noun event notation.

routing.description: The implementation perspective on this operation. Describes the technical execution path: the entry handler, middleware chain, the ordered sequence of function calls and decisions, and error handling. Audience: engineers reading or implementing the code.

handler.description: The name of the primary handler function or method. Use the actual name from the codebase.

file_ref.description: Path to the file containing the handler, relative to the repo root.

middleware_chain.description: Ordered list of middleware, guards, and interceptors applied before the handler runs. Each entry runs in the order listed. These are DL intra-Container Connects that run before the Unit.

name.description: Function or class name of this middleware.

purpose.description: What this middleware does in plain language.

on_failure.description: What happens if this middleware rejects the request.

steps.description: The ordered sequence of function calls, service calls, and decisions inside the handler. This is the DL Flow — the ordered execution path through the Unit. Use hierarchical step ids: '1.0', '2.0' for sequential; '2.a', '2.b' for parallel at the same level.

id.description: Hierarchical step identifier. Sequential: '1.0', '2.0'. Parallel: '2.a', '2.b'. Sub-steps: '2.a.1', '2.a.2'.

action.description: What this step does. Write as a code-level verb phrase describing the call or operation.

calls.description: The actual function, method, or dependency being invoked. Use the name as it appears in the codebase. This is a DL intra-Container Connect.

dependency_ref.description: If this step calls an external dependency, the id of the Dependency primitive. This is a DL inter-Container Connect candidate. Must reference a valid dependency id in dependencies.sdl.json.

parallel.description: When true, this step runs concurrently with other steps sharing the same parent step id level (e.g., '2.a' and '2.b' are parallel).

condition.description: Guard condition for this step, expressed in code terms.

returns.description: What this step returns or produces.

throws.description: The error type thrown on failure.

handling.description: How this error is caught or propagated.
examples: Caught by global error handler, mapped to 409

notes.description: Design rationale or non-obvious implementation notes for this step.

transaction.description: If this operation runs inside a database or distributed transaction, describe its scope.

scope.description: What is included in the transaction boundary.
examples: Order insert + event outbox insert in the same DB transaction

idempotency.description: How this operation handles duplicate invocations.

key.description: The field or combination used as the idempotency key.

input_shape_ref.description: The id of the DataShape representing the validated input to this operation. Must reference a valid data-shape id in data-shapes.sdl.json.

output_shape_ref.description: The id of the DataShape representing what this operation returns on success. Must reference a valid data-shape id in data-shapes.sdl.json.
